metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a formal, exhaustive profile vocabulary (e.g. an enum or named-constant table) for the `profile` parameter of `compute_validity`, listing every valid value and the check flags each enables/disables.
evidence: Plan text: 'The claim/default profile checks code freshness. The close profile sets check_code_freshness=false'
Plan text: 'drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity with the drift-refresh/default profile'
No enum class, string literal table, or named constant set is defined anywhere in the plan for profile values.
Tool search for 'profile' in src/rebar/llm/plan_review returned zero matches — the parameter does not yet exist, and no vocabulary is defined in the plan.
findings.finding: The `profile` parameter added to `compute_validity` has no enumerated value vocabulary. The plan references 'claim/default profile', 'close profile', and 'drift-refresh/default profile' as informal prose labels but never defines the formal string or enum values an implementer must pass for `profile=`. A code author cannot determine whether to write `profile='close'`, `profile='close-profile'`, `profile=Profile.CLOSE`, or something else.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None) with an optional profile parameter'
scenarios: Implementer of the close consumer calls compute_validity with the wrong profile string, silently receiving claim-profile behaviour (code freshness checked when it should not be).

Implementer of drift_refresh_candidate cannot distinguish 'drift-refresh/default' from plain 'default' — these two labels are collapsed in the plan but may need to differ if drift-refresh has a distinct semantics.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section a table such as: `profile=None` (default/claim): all checks; `profile='close'`: signature + registry + own-material + configured-pins, `check_code_freshness=False`; clarify whether 'drift-refresh' is a distinct value or an alias for the default.

findings.finding: The mechanism for AC 'Enabled pin drift prevents progressive drift refresh' is unspecified: `drift_refresh_candidate` (attest.py:146-179) currently returns a non-None candidate based only on material+regver checks — it never checks pin health. Under the new plan, if enforcement is enabled and pins have drifted, `drift_refresh_candidate` would still return a valid candidate and `drift_refresh` would proceed to the probe, violating the AC. The plan does not say whether `drift_refresh_candidate` itself should call `compute_plan_review_validity` before returning, whether `drift_refresh` should call it as a pre-gate, or whether the profile check is threaded through the candidate return value.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'drift refresh' consumer / AC: 'Enabled pin drift prevents progressive drift refresh'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'Before returning a refreshable candidate, `drift_refresh_candidate` calls `compute_plan_review_validity` with the drift-refresh profile (enforcement check enabled); a non-current pin_status causes it to return None (→ full review), consistent with how material/regver mismatches already block the candidate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which `pin_status` values constitute 'non-current' for the purpose of blocking `drift_refresh_candidate` (i.e., enumerate the exact set: stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin — and confirm legacy-unpinned does NOT block drift refresh when enforcement is disabled).
evidence: Plan text: 'enabled non-current pin health returns None' — 'non-current' is not a formal pin_status value; the enum values are current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, legacy-unpinned.
Code confirms drift_refresh_candidate (attest.py:146-179) has no call to compute_validity and no pin-health logic — this is entirely new logic added by this plan.
The plan does not specify whether legacy-unpinned counts as 'non-current' for the purpose of blocking drift refresh.
findings.finding: The processing rule connecting `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status != current` to `drift_refresh_candidate` returning `None` is never formally specified. The plan says 'enabled non-current pin health returns None and forces a full review before any probe', but 'non-current pin health' is informal shorthand — it is never linked to the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status` field or any specific subset of its values (stale-pin-drift vs stale-pin-missing vs malformed-pin).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity with the drift-refresh/default profile; enabled non-current pin health returns None and forces a full review before any probe.'
scenarios: Implementer treats legacy-unpinned as non-current and blocks drift-refresh for all legacy attestations, breaking the safe-rollout guarantee.
Implementer treats only stale-pin-drift as non-current and misses stale-pin-missing/malformed-pin cases, allowing a drift-refresh on a materially broken pin.

findings.finding: The plan specifies that `current_fingerprint` in `TargetPinDetail` is `string|null` but never specifies the fallback decision rule when a pinned target's fingerprint CANNOT BE READ at health-compute time (e.g., due to a read error, not because the target is deleted). The plan states 'archived targets remain readable' and the config fail-open rule, but neither covers a transient read failure on a live related target.
findings.location: Implementation section — TargetPinDetail schema and 'When enabled, drift ... invalidate' paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the 'drift-refresh' profile in compute_validity skips the code-drift check (stale-code) and checks only regver, material, own-material, and configured pins — since code drift is expected and handled by drift_refresh_candidate's own probe logic; distinguish this from 'default profile' in the plan text.
evidence: Current compute_validity plan-review branch (attest.py:570-586) returns stale-code when any signed dep hashes differ — unconditionally, with no profile gate

drift_refresh_candidate (attest.py:146-179) is specifically designed for attestations where deps have drifted; calling compute_validity with the default code-drift check would always return invalid here
The plan says 'enabled non-current pin health returns None' — this implies only pin status is new, but the profile name 'drift-refresh/default' suggests full default validation runs
Plan uses 'default profile' as if it can coexist with a drifted attestation, but the existing default path is stale-code on any dep drift
findings.finding: The plan's 'drift-refresh/default profile' language is mechanically ambiguous and, as written, would break progressive drift refresh: if compute_validity with the default profile is called inside drift_refresh_candidate, the existing code-drift check (stale-code verdict) fires on every drifted attestation — exactly the inputs drift_refresh_candidate is designed to process — causing it to always return None and disabling progressive refresh entirely.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity with the drift-refresh/default profile'
scenarios: Operator enables enforce_plan_material_pins=true; ticket has a drifted-code (stale-code) but pin-current attestation; drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity(profile='drift-refresh') → stale-code → returns None → full review required instead of fast refresh
Same scenario with enforcement disabled: same outcome — progressive refresh is broken regardless of enforcement toggle
findings.suggested_fix: Define the drift-refresh profile as: check_code_freshness=false, check_regver=true, check_material=true, check_pins=true (when enabled). Update the plan to say 'drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity with the drift-refresh profile (code-drift check disabled)' and separately document that the default profile retains the code-drift check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `drift_refresh_candidate`'s pin-enforcement integration is *new logic* (the function currently has no `compute_validity` call), and specify how the existing inline checks relate to the new profile-based call.
evidence: attest.py:146-179: drift_refresh_candidate performs inline checks with no call to compute_validity

Plan text: 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity with the drift-refresh/default profile; enabled non-current pin health returns None and forces a full review before any probe'
findings.finding: The plan says 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity with the drift-refresh/default profile; enabled non-current pin health returns None and forces a full review'. The actual drift_refresh_candidate (attest.py:146-179) does NOT call compute_validity at all — it performs its own inline checks (verify_signature, manifest_regver, manifest_material, manifest_deps, _rehash). This means the described pin-enforcement integration into drift_refresh_candidate is entirely new logic, not a migration of an existing compute_validity call.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity with the drift-refresh/default profile'
scenarios: The implementer may expect a minor wiring change but instead must add a new compute_validity call path inside drift_refresh_candidate, potentially changing its error-handling semantics and the None-return contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'migrate … the close story' framing: there is no existing plan-review `compute_validity` call in `transition_close.py`; specify this as *new gate logic* to add and describe under what config condition the close-path plan-review pin check activates.
evidence: transition_close.py:444-470: only _completion_precheck (completion-verifier gate) is called on close; no plan-review compute_validity call exists
Plan text: 'Migrate the existing plan-review callers … and the close story to this same function'
The phrase 'migrate … the close story' implies an existing plan-review check being moved, but none exists
findings.finding: The plan lists 'the close story' as one of the consumers to 'Migrate … to this same function' (compute_validity with profile parameter). However, transition_close.py contains NO existing plan-review compute_validity call — it only calls compute_validity for completion-verifier via completion.py. There is no 'close-story plan-review consumer' to migrate; this is entirely new gate logic that must be added.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Migrate the existing plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function'
scenarios: Implementer treats this as a simple refactor of an existing close-path call, finds nothing to migrate, and may omit adding the new plan-review gate on close altogether.
AC 2 ('Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer when that gate is active') then has no grounded implementation target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the 'close-profile consumer' / 'close story' is an EXISTING gate being migrated or a NEW gate being created in this ticket; if new, add `transition_close.py` and a new `require_plan_review_for_close` config key explicitly to the implementation/file-impact list.
evidence: Search for 'require_plan_review_for_close', 'close_profile', 'close profile', 'plan-review close gate' all return zero matches
src/rebar/_config_schema.py VerifyConfig has no `require_plan_review_for_close` field
The existing close path in transition_close.py checks plan-review attestation only through `sign_completion_verdict` for the completion-verifier gate — not a plan-review gate
src/rebar/_commands/gates.py only defines plan_review_precheck for in_progress transitions
findings.finding: The plan names a 'close-profile consumer' and 'the close story' as existing consumers of `compute_validity` that need migrating, but no plan-review close gate exists in the codebase — the only plan-review validity check on the close path is the start-work gate (`plan_review_precheck`), not a close gate.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Migrate the existing plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function. The close profile sets check_code_freshness=false'; AC: 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer when that gate is active.'
scenarios: If the 'close story' / 'close-profile consumer' is NEW (not existing), the plan understates scope by calling it migration rather than creation — the AC test 'when that gate is active' implies a gate that must first be built.
If the implementer treats the close-profile gate as already existing, they will search for it and not find it, causing confusion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `check_code_freshness` is a *new* profile-controlled flag to be added to `compute_validity`'s plan-review branch (it does not currently exist), and specify which existing code-drift checks it suppresses and which it leaves active.
evidence: attest.py:471-477: compute_validity signature is `(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None)` — no check_code_freshness parameter
attest.py:543-625: the plan-review branch performs code-drift checks unconditionally with no code_freshness gate
Plan text presents this as a profile property of the extended function, but no such mechanism exists today
findings.finding: The plan says 'the close profile sets check_code_freshness=false while retaining signature, registry, own-material, and configured pin checks'. There is no check_code_freshness parameter in the existing compute_validity function (attest.py:471-627) — the function has no such keyword argument. This is entirely new behavior to introduce, not an existing parameter being toggled.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'The close profile sets check_code_freshness=false while retaining signature, registry, own-material, and configured pin checks'
scenarios: Implementer must design and add check_code_freshness suppression logic to compute_validity's plan-review branch, which requires touching the stale-code / stale-head check paths and their test coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `compute_plan_review_validity` in the contract description is a reference to the existing `compute_validity` function — remove or replace the name `compute_plan_review_validity` to avoid confusion with the actual extension target.
evidence: Search for 'compute_plan_review_validity' returns no matches
attest.py:471 shows `def compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None)`
Plan says 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None)' — confirming the real function is `compute_validity`
The phrase 'beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level' is internally inconsistent with the later correct citation of `compute_validity`
findings.finding: The plan describes `compute_plan_review_validity` as an 'existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}' function, but only `compute_validity` exists in the codebase (attest.py:471). There is no `compute_plan_review_validity` anywhere.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
scenarios: An implementer reading the Implementation section literally could waste time searching for a `compute_plan_review_validity` that does not exist, or accidentally create a NEW function instead of extending the existing `compute_validity`.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}' with 'the health sub-object sits beside the existing {valid, reason, verdict} keys already returned by compute_validity'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a co-located decision rule to the TargetPinDetail schema definition specifying exactly when `current_fingerprint` is null (e.g. null only when the pinned target's ticket cannot be read/found; archived targets that are readable produce a non-null fingerprint).
evidence: Plan text defines the field as `current_fingerprint: string|null` without a nullability rule.
Plan text: 'Archived targets remain readable' — implies archived targets may still have a current_fingerprint, but this is not confirmed.
Plan text: 'When enabled, drift, malformed pins, or a deleted/missing pinned target invalidate' — deleted/missing is one source of null, but 'malformed' and 'archived' cases are not addressed.
No processing rule is co-located with the TargetPinDetail schema definition explaining when null is produced.
findings.finding: The condition under which `TargetPinDetail.current_fingerprint` is `null` vs a non-null string is never specified. The plan defines the field as `current_fingerprint: string|null` but does not state the decision rule: is it null only when the pinned target is missing/deleted (`stale-pin-missing`), or also when the target is archived, or when fingerprint recomputation fails?
findings.location: Implementation section: 'TargetPinDetail is exactly {canonical_id: string, role: child|prerequisite, pinned_fingerprint: string, current_fingerprint: string|null, pin_status: ...}'
scenarios: Implementer produces null for archived targets; renderer treats this as stale-pin-missing and reports a false alarm.
Implementer produces null on fingerprint-recomputation failure; enforcement logic incorrectly treats a transient I/O failure as a missing target.

findings.finding: The plan states two conflicting policies for unreadable configuration: 'keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning' (general rule) and 'once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed' (post-enable rule). The behavior when enforcement has been previously enabled but config becomes transiently unreadable at validation time is unspecified — a consumer could silently bypass enforcement via a transient config-read failure, defeating the closed-failure guarantee.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph: 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning, matching existing gate posture; once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a spec sentence: 'If enforcement was last successfully read as enabled and config subsequently becomes unreadable, validation MUST fail closed (not open); fail-open applies only when enforcement has never been successfully set.' Add a corresponding AC: 'Config unreadable after enforcement was enabled: validation fails closed.'

findings.finding: The `current_fingerprint: string|null` field in TargetPinDetail is defined as null-capable, but the plan gives no decision rule distinguishing the null cases: I/O error on fingerprint recompute vs. target deleted vs. target archived. The enforcement branch ('deleted/missing pinned target invalidate') requires distinguishing deleted from archived, but both would present as null or unreadable without a disambiguation rule.
findings.location: Implementation — TargetPinDetail definition: 'current_fingerprint: string|null'; also 'Archived targets remain readable'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add concrete proving commands to the Verification section — at minimum name the test module(s) and a representative pytest selector (e.g., `pytest tests/attestation/test_pin_health.py -k 'drift_invalidate or legacy_unpinned'`) for each class of AC.
evidence: 'Run focused attestation invalidation, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, and config tests.' — no file path or test selector given
'Run make check and the non-integration test suite.' — generic command that proves no individual AC
findings.finding: The Verification section contains no concrete proving commands — 'Run focused attestation invalidation, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, and config tests' is a description of test categories, not an invocation (no module path, no pytest selector, no make target). An executor cannot run these without stopping to locate and identify the relevant test files.
findings.location: ## Verification
scenarios: An agent completing the plan has no command to run to confirm each AC is satisfied; the test suite may pass entirely without new coverage for the added code paths.
findings.suggested_fix:
Under Verification, replace the category descriptions with explicit commands such as:
```
pytest tests/attestation/test_pin_health.py -k 'drift_invalidate or reuse or refresh or resign or audit or legacy'
pytest tests/attestation/test_pin_health.py -k 'advisory_no_valid_change'
make check
```

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the close profile sets check_code_freshness=false while retaining signature, registry, own-material, and configured pin checks' but no parameter or attribute named check_code_freshness exists anywhere in the codebase (zero grep matches in src/). The close consumer in transition_close.py calls compute_validity with no such parameter, and compute_validity's signature at attest.py:471 has no such keyword argument. This is an unbacked implementation assertion about a specific named interface element that must be invented from scratch, yet is stated as if it is an existing knob to toggle.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'The close profile sets check_code_freshness=false while retaining signature, registry, own-material, and configured pin checks'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/audit/read.py` and `src/rebar/audit/page.py` to the edit-target list if they need to surface `DerivedPlanReviewHealth`; or explicitly state they receive no changes (the health field flows through the existing plan_reviews data).
evidence: src/rebar/audit/read.py: audit_trail() aggregates plan-review history; audit/page.py shapes it for the web UI
Neither file appears in any edit-target enumeration in the plan
search for 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth' returns no matches — these files currently have no pin-health rendering
AC: 'Audit reports enabled pin drift as invalid' and 'Derived health reports enabled pin drift as invalid' — audit consumers must surface the new contract
findings.finding: `src/rebar/audit/read.py` and `src/rebar/audit/page.py` are named as implicit 'renderer' consumers of the new `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract ('all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings'), but neither file is called out as an edit target.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings'
scenarios: The audit trail UI could silently display stale/inconsistent health data if audit/read.py and audit/page.py are not updated to consume DerivedPlanReviewHealth.
AC 'Audit reports enabled pin drift as invalid' cannot pass if the audit layer does not read the new health field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and name the specific file/function constituting the 'audit' consumer (likely `audit/read.py:audit_trail` or `audit/page.py`) and add it to the Implementation's consumer migration list, since it currently has no compute_validity call path.
evidence: audit/read.py: AuditTrail reads plan_reviews from sidecar.all_review_results(), not from compute_validity or any attestation validity check
audit/page.py: _plan_section() and _plan_round() render sidecar payload fields only; no attestation validity fields are present

ABSENCE: the plan names 'audit/health' as a consumer to migrate but does not identify which file/function is the 'audit' consumer or describe what changes are needed to make the audit path call compute_validity with the new profile
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'all renderers consume this contract' (DerivedPlanReviewHealth) but the audit renderer (`audit/read.py`, `audit/page.py`) has no current path that reads plan-review attestation validity — it reads only sidecar review results. This capability assertion is unverified against the actual audit architecture.
findings.location: Implementation — 'all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings'; AC: 'Audit reports enabled pin drift as invalid'
scenarios: The audit consumer (audit/read.py or audit/page.py) may need substantial new wiring to call compute_validity and surface DerivedPlanReviewHealth — not just a 'migrate to the same function' change
AC 'Audit reports enabled pin drift as invalid' may require new file_impact entries and implementation steps not currently in the plan

findings.finding: The plan names `audit/health` as one of the six consumers of `compute_plan_review_validity`, but no existing audit-health endpoint or function for plan-review pin health can be found in the codebase. If this is a new surface, the file targets for it (e.g. `src/rebar/audit/page.py`, audit CLI, or a new module) are not enumerated.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by … audit/health'

findings.finding: The plan names `compute_plan_review_validity` as the unified boundary, but this symbol does not exist — the codebase has `compute_validity` (in `attest.py`) which already serves plan-review and completion-verifier. The plan does not specify whether `compute_plan_review_validity` replaces, wraps, or is renamed from `compute_validity`, leaving the migration path for all existing callers (including `completion.py`, `_attested_delivered`, `drift_refresh_candidate`, `claim_gate_check` in `attest.py`) ambiguous.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close.'

findings.finding: The plan introduces `compute_plan_review_validity` as a new named profile boundary but never specifies its relationship to the existing `compute_validity` function confirmed in attest.py:471 (which has 6+ confirmed call-sites including completion.py:176 outside plan_review/). No disposition is stated: is `compute_plan_review_validity` a rename, a wrapper, or a replacement? An implementer reading both the plan and the code cannot determine how to migrate existing call-sites.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary' paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the omnibus 'deterministic' AC with explicit expected-outcome statements for each sub-scenario: state what pin_status and valid value each of deleted/missing, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable returns in both enabled and disabled modes.
evidence: 'duplicate' does not appear in the Implementation section at all — no expected pin_status, no aggregation rule

're-enable' scenario expected behavior is not described (e.g., does switching enforce_plan_material_pins from false to true immediately invalidate previously-valid attestations? The plan says 'do not rewrite pins when configuration changes' but says nothing about re-validation on re-enable)
'deterministic' is not an observable system state — it describes the nature of the implementation rather than what the system emits
findings.finding: The AC 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic' does not state a specific observable outcome — 'deterministic' is not an outcome. Furthermore, 'duplicate' (duplicate pin entries in a manifest) and 're-enable' (toggling enforcement back on after it was off) scenarios are never given expected results in the Implementation section, so an executor cannot know what output to assert.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic'
scenarios: An executor writes a test for duplicate pins but guesses the wrong expected pin_status (e.g., malformed-pin vs stale-pin-drift vs a new error)
An executor handling the re-enable toggle does not know whether previously cached 'valid' verdicts are immediately invalidated or only at next call
findings.suggested_fix:
Split into specific ACs, e.g.:
- Enabled duplicate-pin manifest returns malformed-pin / valid=false at every consumer.
- Re-enabling enforcement (false→true) causes the next compute_validity call to evaluate pinned state afresh (no cached valid verdict persists).
- Archived target returns its last-known pin_status and valid=true regardless of enforcement mode.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit Implementation sentence stating that `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` controls validation only and has no effect on pin signing/emission, so ACs 10–11 have grounded described work.
evidence: 'Add verify.enforce_plan_material_pins with default false and strict enabled-mode validation' — no mention of emission
'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled' — no Implementation text covers this
ABSENCE: the Implementation never mentions that pin signing/emission is independent of the enforcement flag
findings.finding: ACs 10 and 11 ('verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled' and '=false leaves new pin emission enabled') have no corresponding described work in the Implementation section. The Implementation describes verify.enforce_plan_material_pins only as controlling enforcement/validation; it says nothing about the flag's relationship to pin emission. These ACs test a distinct behavioral invariant (emission independence) that is nowhere specified in the Implementation body.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria lines 10–11; Implementation section (verify.enforce_plan_material_pins description)
scenarios: An implementer might reasonably implement enforcement by disabling emission when enforcement is off, failing these ACs
A reviewer cannot verify these ACs against any described design decision
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section under the verify.enforce_plan_material_pins bullet, add: 'This flag controls validation enforcement only; pin emission (signing) is always active regardless of its value.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'malformed manifest metadata' in the final AC covers (a) malformed pin fingerprints only, or (b) also a structurally malformed attestation manifest envelope, and add the broader case to the Implementation if intended.
evidence: Implementation: 'TargetPinDetail is exactly {canonical_id: string, role: child|prerequisite, pinned_fingerprint: string, current_fingerprint: string|null, pin_status: current|stale-pin-drift|stale-pin-missing|malformed-pin}'
AC: 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising' — 'manifest metadata' ≠ 'pin fingerprint'
ABSENCE: no Implementation text describes behavior when the attestation's manifest envelope/metadata itself is structurally malformed
findings.finding: The AC 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising' uses the phrase 'malformed manifest metadata', but the Implementation defines malformed-pin as a specific pin_status value for a malformed pin fingerprint. The AC's broader phrase could mean the attestation's own manifest metadata is unparseable/structurally malformed — a distinct scenario not described in the Implementation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (last bullet); Implementation section (TargetPinDetail / DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract)
scenarios: If 'manifest metadata' means the attestation document's metadata block is unparseable, no implementation guidance exists for that path
A tester writing the verification for this AC cannot determine what to malform

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback or expand-contract strategy for the caller migration (e.g., 'callers pass profile=None initially and are cut over one at a time; revert by removing the profile argument to restore prior behavior').
evidence: 'Migrate the existing plan-review callers...' — behavioral change to six existing consumers
No rollback or back-out strategy described anywhere in the plan
No expand-contract sequence described (e.g., shadow-run new profile alongside old before cutting over)
findings.finding: The plan migrates multiple existing callers (claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, close story) to pass a new profile parameter through compute_validity. These callers are existing, relied-upon code paths. The plan provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequencing should the migration introduce a regression across any of these consumers.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Migrate the existing plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function.'
scenarios: A bug in the profile-parameter dispatch silently changes validity results for claim_gate_check or close without a described recovery path
Partial migration (some callers migrated, some not) leaves the system in an inconsistent state with no described detection or recovery
findings.suggested_fix: Add a rollback note: 'Each caller migration is independently revertable by removing the profile keyword argument, restoring the existing default-path behavior. Callers can be migrated and verified one at a time before the next.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract shape, severity-aggregation ordering, and compute_validity profile-dispatch pattern in an ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design doc so future consumers (including child 5967) have a canonical reference.
evidence: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(...) with an optional profile parameter'
No ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update is mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel contract (DerivedPlanReviewHealth with severity-ordered aggregation, TargetPinDetail shape, and profile-based dispatch on compute_validity) and a new cross-consumer pattern, but names no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc capture for these architectural decisions.
findings.location: Implementation section — DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract definition; compute_validity profile parameter
scenarios: A future contributor adding a new consumer (e.g., child 5967's phase_status) has no canonical reference for the severity ordering, null-vs-empty-list semantics, or profile dispatch contract
The profile pattern on compute_validity is non-obvious; without a recorded decision, it is likely to be re-litigated or implemented inconsistently
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief 'Architecture' subsection or ADR entry recording: (1) TargetPinDetail field semantics and null vs. empty-list conventions, (2) severity ordering rationale (malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current), (3) why profile dispatch was chosen over a separate function, and (4) the extension point for child 5967's phase_status.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the deploy ordering between this plan and child 5967, and explicitly state that `phase_status` is an additive nullable field so consumers of this plan's contract are forward-compatible with child 5967's extension.
evidence: 'Child 5967 extends the same contract with phase_status and phase compatibility after it introduces the phase manifest format'
ABSENCE: no statement that phase_status is optional/nullable in the interim, and no deploy-ordering constraint between this story and child 5967
findings.finding: The deploy ordering between this plan and child 5967 is unspecified. Child 5967 extends the same `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract with `phase_status` and phase compatibility. Consumers introduced in this plan will encounter a contract without those fields for an indeterminate window; the coexistence and compatibility expectations are not documented.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Child 5967 extends the same contract with phase_status and phase compatibility after it introduces the phase manifest format'
scenarios: If child 5967 consumers read phase_status before child 5967 is deployed, they receive a contract missing that field; if child 5967 is deployed before this plan, its phase_status extension has no base contract to extend.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in Implementation: 'This plan's health contract must be deployed before child 5967. The phase_status field added by child 5967 is optional/nullable; consumers built against this plan must tolerate its absence.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a motivation section (or a brief Expected-vs-Actual) showing what the CURRENT behavior does when pin drift occurs — e.g., 'currently drift_refresh_candidate proceeds even when pins are stale because compute_validity does not check pins; this plan adds that check' — so the agent can verify necessity before building.
evidence: 'Use signed pins as the single related-material validity check across every attestation lifecycle consumer' — asserts the desired end state without explaining what the current behavior does instead.
No Reproduction, no Expected/Actual, no named defect, no user/system problem caused by the current behavior is cited anywhere in the plan.
'preserving safe rollout for existing projects' implies a risk but not a demonstrated current defect.
findings.finding: The plan proposes adding a config flag, a new health contract, and migrating multiple consumers to pin-drift enforcement, but never establishes that the current behavior is deficient: it does not reproduce a scenario where pin drift goes undetected, name a specific gap in the existing validity checks, or show an Expected-vs-Actual for any current consumer. The motivation is asserted ('use signed pins as the single validity check') without demonstrating the status quo is wrong.
findings.location: ## Goal and ## Implementation sections
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Current behavior / gap' section before the Implementation: name one or two concrete cases where the current system fails to enforce drift (e.g., which consumer currently skips pin checks, or what a user observes today when a plan target is modified after attestation), and tie each Implementation step to closing that specific gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the `profile` parameter abstraction is justified at ≥3 use-sites in THIS story, or replace it with a direct `check_code_freshness: bool = True` keyword argument to `compute_validity` that describes the actual behavioral distinction without requiring a named dispatch concept.
evidence: compute_validity exists at src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:471 with no 'profile' parameter today
Plan names exactly 2 profiles: 'claim/default' and 'close' — Rule-of-Three requires ≥3 before abstracting the dispatch axis
Child 5967 is cited as adding a third use only hypothetically ('child 5967 later adds phase compatibility to both profiles') — future-ticket justification is a YAGNI signal
The actual behavioral difference between profiles is a single boolean (code-freshness on/off), which could be expressed as a direct kwarg rather than a named dispatch concept
findings.finding: The `profile` abstraction added to `compute_validity` has exactly 2 concrete use-sites as specified in this plan (the default/claim profile and the close profile); child 5967 is cited as a future third. Under Rule-of-Three, a new dispatch axis with only 2 live instantiations in this story is premature abstraction — a direct boolean kwarg (e.g., `check_code_freshness: bool = True`) would express the same semantics with no forward-reference dependency.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(…) with an optional profile parameter used only when kind=plan-review; … The claim/default profile checks code freshness. The close profile sets check_code_freshness=false'
scenarios: Implementer introduces a profile enum or string constant with 2 values and no third consumer until child 5967 ships, creating an abstraction that exists solely for a future story
If child 5967 is deferred or cancelled, the profile concept is stranded with only 2 use-sites indefinitely
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `profile` with an explicit `check_code_freshness: bool = True` kwarg on `compute_validity`. The close caller passes `check_code_freshness=False`; the claim/default caller uses the default. Child 5967 can add its own kwarg (e.g., `check_phase_compat: bool = False`) when it ships. This avoids the premature profile dispatch axis while preserving the same semantics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state that `manifest_pins()` and the `plan-material-pin:` manifest-line format from story acaf-bb4d are hard prerequisites, and add a verification step confirming those symbols exist before this story's implementation begins.
evidence: manifest.py: no manifest_pins function or any 'plan-material-pin' prefix defined
Search for 'manifest_pins' and 'plan-material-pin' returns only cache files from story acaf-bb4d (a predecessor story), not any live source file
Plan text: 'Use signed pins as the single related-material validity check across every attestation lifecycle consumer'
findings.finding: The plan says 'Compare signed pins against current narrow material to populate it' (the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract), treating signed pins as already parseable from the manifest. However, manifest.py has no manifest_pins() function — the only parsers present are manifest_deps, manifest_regver, manifest_material, manifest_rebar_version, manifest_disabled_builtins. The signed-pin infrastructure is a dependency from a predecessor story (acaf-bb4d), not a codebase fact.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Compare signed pins against current narrow material to populate it; all renderers consume this contract'
scenarios: If the predecessor story's pin-emission infrastructure has not landed, this story has no signed pins to compare against, and the entire validity-check mechanism has no foundation to build on.
The plan should explicitly call out the predecessor story as a hard dependency and verify its deliverables exist before any implementation begins.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py` to the explicit file-impact / edit-target list, since it must call `compute_validity` with the appropriate profile to enforce pin drift.
evidence: resign.py: searches confirm the file never calls `compute_validity`
resign.py lines 51–130 perform their own validity checks (material fingerprint match) without calling `compute_validity`
The plan enumerates 're-sign' as a caller to migrate but does not name resign.py as an edit target
No file impact entry mentions resign.py
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py` is listed as a named consumer ('re-sign') that must be migrated to call `compute_validity` with a profile, but the file is not named as an edit target and currently calls `attest.current_material_fingerprint` and `sidecar.latest_review_result` directly without going through `compute_validity` at all.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Migrate the existing plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function.'; AC: 'Enabled pin drift prevents re-sign.'
scenarios: Without updating resign.py, enabled pin drift would NOT prevent re-sign, violating AC 'Enabled pin drift prevents re-sign'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for the compute_validity + profile approach: specifically, that a single enforcement point prevents consumer divergence and that an optional profile parameter avoids duplicating the base validation logic across close-profile and claim-profile callers.
evidence: Plan is purely descriptive: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(...) with an optional profile parameter' — no 'because' or 'in order to avoid X'
Plan does not state why a profile parameter is better than a separate function or a separate compute_pin_health call at each site
Absence of rationale is itself a G6 finding per rubric: 'The plan states a POSITIVE rationale for the chosen approach (its absence is a finding)'
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for choosing the compute_validity + optional profile parameter approach over structurally different alternatives (e.g., a standalone compute_pin_health function called independently at each consumer site, or separate close-profile validity function). The rationale absence leaves implementers without guidance on why this design was selected.
findings.location: Implementation section (entire)
scenarios: Future maintainer or reviewer cannot determine why the profile parameter approach was chosen and may refactor toward a separate function, introducing divergence
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Implementation section: 'This approach is chosen because a single compute_validity entry point with profile-controlled checks prevents consumer logic from diverging, and an optional profile parameter avoids duplicating base validation (signature, registry, own-material) across the close and claim paths.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify ownership of the full DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract shape: either (a) acknowledge the parent explicitly assigns phase_status ownership to 5967 and update the leaf to reference 'the contract is completed by 5967 per parent design,' or (b) update the parent to formally authorize the split-delivery model so the leaf's interim shape is parent-approved.
evidence: Parent (jira-reb-1160) Enforcement/rollout section: 'That nested contract has pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned; phase_status exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed; enforced boolean; and targets: list[TargetPinDetail].' — phase_status is part of the single unified contract, not a later addition.

Leaf says 'Child 5967 extends the same contract' — this frames 5967 as an extender of a contract the leaf defines, but the parent assigns the complete contract definition to compute_plan_review_validity without authorizing the leaf to ship an incomplete interim shape.

Parent AC: 'All detailed review surfaces consume the shared derived-health contract.' — if the leaf ships health without phase_status and surfaces are built against it before 5967 lands, the surfaces will not conform to the parent-defined contract.
findings.finding: The leaf defines DerivedPlanReviewHealth without phase_status, deferring it to sibling 5967 as an 'extension.' The parent defines the full contract — including phase_status (exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed) — as part of compute_plan_review_validity from the outset, without authorizing a split delivery.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract … Child 5967 extends the same contract with phase_status and phase compatibility after it introduces the phase manifest format.'
scenarios: If 5967 is delayed or descoped, the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract the leaf ships will permanently lack phase_status, contradicting the parent's defined schema.
Any surface or consumer built against the leaf's partial contract before 5967 lands will need a second migration, creating integration churn not anticipated by the parent.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the leaf's Implementation section referencing the parent's decomposition: 'Per the parent epic's decomposition, phase_status is owned by story 5967; the contract shape emitted by this story is intentionally partial until 5967 lands.' Then confirm the parent's AC for surfaces consuming the 'shared derived-health contract' either gates on 5967 completing first, or explicitly accounts for the two-phase delivery.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art note in the Implementation section explaining what staleness the new signed-pin check catches that the existing `material_fingerprint` (which already incorporates children via `orchestrator.material_fingerprint`) does NOT — e.g. prerequisite-ticket drift, or relationship-graph changes that fall outside the current material hash.
evidence: attest.py:769-795: `current_material_fingerprint` delegates to `orchestrator.material_fingerprint` which already incorporates children
The plan never states what 'narrow material' covers that the existing material fingerprint does not
ADR 0002 already established content-addressed staleness for code files; the new pin mechanism appears to replicate that pattern for ticket-graph relations without citing the prior art
ABSENCE: no section in the plan contrasts the new pin-fingerprint approach against the existing material_fingerprint to justify the separate mechanism
findings.finding: The plan introduces signed related-ticket pins as a new staleness mechanism without explaining what gap the existing `material_fingerprint` (which already hashes ticket + children via `orchestrator.material_fingerprint`) does not cover. Children's compiled state already flows into `current_material_fingerprint`; the plan may be reinventing or duplicating that mechanism for a subset of the same concern.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Compare signed pins against current narrow material to populate it'
scenarios: If child ticket description/AC changes already invalidate via material_fingerprint, pin staleness for the same child is redundant — two mechanisms fighting over the same concern with potentially different invalidation timing
If 'narrow material' is a SUBSET of what material_fingerprint covers, the plan is adding enforcement complexity for no additional safety coverage
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that 'narrow material' covers prerequisite (depends_on/blocks) ticket state, which is explicitly NOT included in the current `material_fingerprint` (which only hashes the ticket's own description/AC/file_impact + direct children). If that is the intended scope, state it explicitly in the Implementation section so the boundary is clear.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the parallel `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` struct vs extending `compute_validity`'s existing return dict with pin health fields (e.g. `pin_status`, `enforced`, `targets`) — or cite an explicit reason (e.g. backward-compat with callers that destructure the top-level dict) that makes the two-level shape the right call.
evidence: ADR 0009 establishes compute_validity as the single place that decides whether an attestation is currently valid, returning {valid, reason, verdict}
The plan adds a parallel `health` key rather than extending the existing return dict or an established observer pattern
ABSENCE: no rationale is given for why health is a separate struct rather than additional fields in the existing return dict (e.g. `pin_status`, `targets` keys alongside `valid`/`reason`/`verdict`)
findings.finding: The plan proposes `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` as a parallel return contract beside `compute_validity`'s existing `{valid, reason, verdict}` dict without justifying this novel structural choice over enriching the existing return dict — a potential anti-repackaging violation against the established ADR 0009 computed-on-read pattern.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
scenarios: Consumers that only check `.get('valid')` will silently ignore pin drift when enforcement is disabled unless they also inspect the parallel health struct
The two-struct return shape is a novel pattern not seen elsewhere in the codebase, requiring all six named consumers to be updated to unpack both levels

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact return shape of `compute_validity` after extension: either `{valid, reason, verdict, health: DerivedPlanReviewHealth}` as a new key in the existing dict, or document an alternative return convention, so all consumers and renderers can be implemented consistently.
evidence: Plan text: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
Plan text: 'do not rename or wrap it' (referring to compute_validity) — rules out a new wrapper function returning a tuple.
Confirmed via code read (attest.py:471-627): compute_validity currently returns exactly {valid, reason, verdict} — a plain dict. The plan never specifies that a 'health' key is added to this dict.
Plan text: 'all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings' — renderers need a stable, specified key name.
findings.finding: The return-type contract of `compute_validity` after the `profile` extension is never specified. The plan says health is 'returned as health beside … existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}', but never states whether `health` is added as a new key to the existing return dict, returned as a second value, or returned via a separate call. This is a structural gap in the schema that every consumer and renderer depends on.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
scenarios: One implementer adds `health` as a key in the return dict; another calls a separate `compute_pin_health()` function. Renderers written to one convention break under the other.
AC 'Derived health reports enabled pin drift as invalid' cannot be tested without knowing which return shape carries the health object.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'compute_validity returns {valid: bool, reason: str, verdict: str, health: DerivedPlanReviewHealth} for kind=plan-review; health is always present (never None) regardless of enforcement mode; for other kinds health is absent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific renderer modules/functions that must be migrated to consume `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` rather than duplicating pin-status strings.
evidence: Plan text: 'all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings' — no renderer names, module paths, or file locations are cited.
No search results for 'renderer' or 'renderers' in src/rebar/llm/plan_review yielded a canonical renderer list.
The plan names six consumers (claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, close story) but does not enumerate the renderer layer separately.
findings.finding: 'All renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings' is an instruction to update an unspecified set of renderer files, with no names or locations given. This is an instruction-locality gap: the implementer cannot determine which files need to change.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings'
scenarios: Implementer updates claim-path renderer but misses an audit/health renderer, causing inconsistent advisory message formatting.
A renderer is added in a future story and duplicates the string constants this story was supposed to centralize.

findings.finding: The plan defines `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status` with five values (current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, legacy-unpinned) but provides no aggregation rule specifying how per-target `TargetPinDetail.pin_status` values roll up into the top-level `pin_status` field. For example: when one target is `stale-pin-drift` and another is `malformed-pin`, which top-level status wins? The rule 'legacy-unpinned has an empty targets list' only covers that one variant.
findings.location: Implementation section — DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract definition
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract definition: 'Top-level pin_status is the highest-severity status across all targets in priority order: malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current; legacy-unpinned is only possible when the targets list is empty.'

findings.finding: The plan states 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close' as though compute_plan_review_validity is the existing boundary, but the actual codebase boundary is compute_validity (attest.py:471) with 10+ external call-sites including rebar/llm/completion.py:176 (outside plan_review/) using kind='completion-verifier'. The plan never names compute_validity, never states it will be renamed/wrapped/replaced, and never addresses the completion-verifier call-site that uses the same function. This makes the relationship between the proposed compute_plan_review_validity and the existing compute_validity unspecified.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close'

findings.finding: The plan introduces `compute_plan_review_validity` as a new named boundary but the codebase already has `compute_validity` at `attest.py:471`, called by `claim_gate_check`, `_idempotent_reuse`, `drift_floor_candidate`, `_attested_delivered`, and the completion path. The plan does not explain why `compute_validity` cannot be extended with a profile/mode parameter instead of being replaced or aliased, nor does it state the migration path for all existing call-sites. This is a novel-architecture decision with no prior-art justification vs the obvious alternative (extend the existing function).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close.'

findings.finding: The AC 'No warning event is persisted for derived health' has no corresponding described deliverable in the Implementation section; no part of the Implementation describes event-persistence behavior for derived health, making this an orphan criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'No warning event is persisted for derived health'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC item (and corresponding test) verifying that disabling `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` after it was active restores `valid=true` and advisory-only reporting for drift cases, confirming the flag is a clean two-way toggle.
evidence: 'once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed' — no corresponding AC tests the reverse transition
AC covers 'Legacy unpinned attestations remain valid across configuration toggles' but not 'drift-pinned attestations return to advisory when enforcement is disabled'
ABSENCE: no AC or verification step names a enabled→disabled rollback test
findings.finding: The plan has no explicit, tested rollback path for the transition enabled→disabled. The phrase 'once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed' could be read as a one-way ratchet. No AC item verifies that disabling enforcement after it was active restores advisory behavior (valid=true for drift cases), leaving the rollback scenario untested.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning … once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed'; Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: An operator enables enforcement, discovers a false-positive pin mismatch, and disables enforcement to unblock CI — the plan gives no assurance this restores valid=true without manual intervention.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'Disabling enforcement (enforce_plan_material_pins=false) on an attestation that was invalid due to drift restores valid=true with drift reported as advisory.' Include a test in the config-toggle test group that runs the full enabled→drift-invalid→disabled→advisory cycle.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly note that resign_plan_review requires a new compute_validity call (or an explicit pin_health check) injected before attest.sign_plan_review, since no such call currently exists in resign.py — this is a structural addition, not a migration of an existing call.
evidence: resign_plan_review (resign.py:35-148) checks recorded_material != current_material then calls attest.sign_plan_review directly — no compute_validity invocation exists
AC: 'Enabled pin drift prevents re-sign' requires a validity check before signing, but no such check currently exists in resign.py
claim_gate_check, _idempotent_reuse, and drift_refresh are genuine migrations (they already call or flow through compute_validity); resign.py is a new integration point
findings.finding: The plan says 'Migrate the existing plan-review callers ... re-sign ... to this same function' but resign_plan_review (resign.py) currently has no compute_validity call — it directly checks material fingerprint and calls sign_plan_review. This is a net-new call-site injection, not a migration, and the plan's characterization conceals that resign.py needs a structural addition (adding a compute_validity call before signing) rather than a simple routing change.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Migrate the existing plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function'
scenarios: Implementation treats resign.py as a simple routing update; developer misses that a new compute_validity call must be added before sign_plan_review; pin drift does not prevent re-sign; AC fails

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define observability (structured log entry or metric counter) for the current_fingerprint=null path and any fingerprint-computation failure, distinguishing infra errors from genuine missing targets, so operators can diagnose false drift invalidations.
evidence: 'No warning event is persisted for derived health' (AC item 13) — removes the one lightweight instrumentation mechanism
'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning' — only the config path has a warning; no mention of metrics/logs/traces for fingerprint-computation failures
TargetPinDetail defines current_fingerprint: string|null implying a failure-to-compute path exists, but no observability is specified for it
findings.finding: New pin-comparison failure paths (e.g., I/O error computing current narrow material for a non-archived target, fingerprint computation failure) are not instrumented beyond the single in-process warning for unreadable config; AC item 13 explicitly prohibits persisting warning events for derived health, leaving these paths dark to operators.
findings.location: AC line: 'No warning event is persisted for derived health'; Implementation: 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning'
scenarios: A target file becomes transiently unreadable mid-attestation lifecycle; current_fingerprint silently returns null; pin_status is reported as stale-pin-missing; operator has no log entry or metric to distinguish a genuine drift from an infra blip
Enforcement is enabled; a file-permission error causes a false 'invalid' verdict; no alert fires; the engineer sees only the end-consumer rejection with no diagnostic trail
findings.suggested_fix: Emit a structured warning log (not a persisted event) at the point fingerprint computation returns null due to an I/O error, tagged with canonical_id and error class, so it is visible in log aggregation without violating the 'no persisted warning event' AC constraint.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
